content.text: React has been in production since 2013 and has gone through three distinct eras of development — class components, hooks, and concurrent rendering. Each era brought fundamental changes to how React apps are structured and what React is capable of. Understanding this history helps you read older codebases, know which patterns are current, and understand
the API looks the way it does today.
React was open-sourced by Facebook in May 2013. Its early versions (0.x through v15) used
as the primary building block. State lived in
, lifecycle methods were overridden on the class (
), and functional components existed but were purely presentational — they could not hold state or run side effects.
Class components worked, but had real friction:
binding was a frequent source of bugs, logic related to the same concern was split across multiple lifecycle methods (e.g. setting up an event listener in
), and reusing stateful logic required awkward patterns like higher-order components (HOCs) or render props.
React 16 was a full internal rewrite of React's reconciler, codenamed
) stayed the same, but internally React now represented work as a linked list of "fiber" units that could be paused, prioritised, and resumed. This was the architectural foundation that made
React 16 also delivered several long-requested user-facing features:
— components that catch render errors in their subtree via
) — render a component's output into a different DOM node, enabling modals and tooltips that escape CSS
— return multiple elements from a component without a wrapping DOM node (
— replaced the old experimental context with
and a Consumer/Provider model, making global state sharing practical without third-party libraries.
— code-split components at the import boundary with a declarative
— the single most impactful API change in React's history. Hooks let functional components use state (
), and let developers extract reusable stateful logic into
— plain JavaScript functions — without changing the component tree.
The same component is shorter, has no
binding, and collocates the state and the side effect that depends on it. More importantly, the
logic can now be extracted into a
hook and shared across any component — something that was impossible to do cleanly with lifecycle methods.

— class components still work in React 19. But within two years of the hooks release, the React community had largely converged on functional components with hooks as the default. All new React features from 2019 onward are hooks-first.
The Built-in Hooks at a Glance
Mutable value that survives re-renders; DOM refs
Mark updates as non-urgent; keep UI responsive
Defer a derived value update until idle
Read a Promise or Context value during render

React 17 was unusual: its release notes led with "No new features." Its purpose was to reduce the friction of incremental framework upgrades — a large app could have one subtree running React 16 and another running React 17 simultaneously, enabling teams to migrate piecemeal rather than all at once.
Event delegation moved from document to root.
Previously React attached all synthetic events to
. In v17 they attach to the React root container, making multiple React versions on one page safe.
at the top of every JSX file. The compiler inserts the import automatically.
React 18 shipped in March 2022. It activated the

capabilities that the Fiber rewrite had made possible five years earlier. Concurrent React can interrupt a render in progress, prioritise urgent updates (like typing) over slower ones (like loading a list), and resume work without visible jank.
calls inside async callbacks (setTimeout, fetch handlers) each triggered a separate render. In React 18, they are automatically batched into one render regardless of context.
Marks a state update as "non-urgent", letting React keep the current UI interactive while computing the next state in the background.
The server can stream HTML for Suspense boundaries as data becomes available, rather than waiting for the entire page to be ready.
A low-priority version of a value — useful for making search/filter inputs stay responsive while a heavy list re-renders in the background.
React 19 (stable release December 2024) is the biggest architectural addition since hooks. It introduced

— components that run exclusively on the server and never ship their code to the browser. Server Components can access databases, file systems, and secrets directly, then send their rendered output to the client as a serialised component tree.
The split between Server Components and Client Components is the defining architecture of React 19. Components are Server Components by default; add
at the top of a file to make it — and everything it imports — a Client Component:
Form submissions can call async server functions via
Read a Promise or Context value during rendering — works with Suspense to suspend until data is ready.
is no longer needed — refs can be passed as a regular prop.
let components declare their resource dependencies so the browser can load them as early as possible.
A build-time compiler that automatically adds
optimisations, aiming to eliminate manual memoisation entirely.
What You Will Encounter in the Wild
When you join a team or read a React tutorial, the version used matters enormously. Here is what to expect:
Legacy codebase; still works but not recommended for new code
Old JSX transform; not needed since v17
Synchronous render; concurrent mode not active
Server Components architecture; file is a Client Component
Deprecated in 19; pass ref as a regular prop now

One of React's strongest qualities is its disciplined approach to backwards compatibility. The React team has a policy of providing codemods (automated refactoring scripts) for breaking changes and deprecating old APIs via warnings for at least one major version before removing them. Class components introduced in 2013 still work in React 19.
This means upgrading React major versions is typically much lower risk than upgrading other frameworks. The React team explicitly commits to a semver-respecting
policy for public APIs. React 18 initially shipped as an opt-in by requiring the
API so existing apps could upgrade without enabling concurrent features accidentally.

is the current stable release and the right choice. If you are starting with a meta-framework, Next.js 14+ and Remix 2+ both support React 19. If you need SSR and are not using a meta-framework, React 18 is still a safe and widely-supported baseline.

, all the fundamentals (JSX, hooks, component composition, useEffect, context) are stable since React 16.8 and unchanged in React 19. You will apply them identically regardless of which version a future employer uses.

React's version history is not just trivia — it explains why you will find useState but not class lifecycle methods in modern code, why forwardRef is being removed, and why 'use client' exists at all. Knowing the eras lets you date code at a glance.
The Pattern: Stable Core, Additive Evolution

Looking across the three eras, a pattern emerges: React never changes the fundamental model, it extends it. JSX, component composition, and unidirectional data flow are identical in React 0.14 and React 19. Each era added a more expressive primitive on top:
Class components gave stateful lifecycle. Hooks abstracted that into composable functions.
Fiber gave incremental rendering. Concurrent Mode exposed priority scheduling to developers.
Client rendering was the only model. Server Components added a server-first rendering tier without removing the client model.

This pattern means that mastering hooks today is not wasted effort when Server Components become mainstream — hooks remain exactly how you write interactive client-side logic. The mental model accumulates; it does not replace itself.
Reading Old React Code: Practical Translation Guide
When you encounter pre-hooks React code in a legacy codebase, you will need to recognise what class-component patterns map to. Here are the most common translations:
Not needed — arrow functions in functional components close over state directly
Custom hooks (same logic, no wrapper component)

has been React's way of surfacing bugs that only appear with concurrent rendering. In development mode, StrictMode deliberately double-invokes render functions and effects to catch side effects that are not properly cleaned up. React 18 made this stricter: effects are now mounted, unmounted, and remounted once to verify cleanup.
Do not disable StrictMode to make the double-invoke stop — fix the underlying issue instead. The double-invoke is a
Common Pitfalls When Reading Version-Mixed Documentation
Older blog posts may use componentWillReceiveProps / componentWillMount.
These were deprecated in React 16.3 and removed in React 18. If you see them, the post is from before 2018.
Many tutorials use CRA as a setup tool. It is no longer maintained. Use Vite (
) or a meta-framework like Next.js for new projects.
import React from 'react' at the top of JSX files.
Required before React 17, not needed after. Both styles compile correctly in modern toolchains.
, it is targeting React ≤ 17. Swap it for
Next.js App Router vs Pages Router.
Next.js 13+ introduced a new App Router based on Server Components alongside the older Pages Router. Tutorials mixing the two are a common source of confusion.
How Each Version Affected Testing Practices
React's evolution also changed how testing is done. In the class component era,

(by Airbnb) was the dominant testing library — it let you shallow-render a component (rendering only that component, not its children) and assert on internal state. This worked for class components but coupled tests to implementation details.
When hooks arrived, Enzyme's shallow rendering broke —
in shallow mode did not behave like in a real browser.

, which always fully renders components and tests from the user's perspective, became the community standard. RTL's philosophy — "The more your tests resemble the way your software is used, the more confidence they can give you" — matched hooks' functional model naturally.
React 18's concurrent features required RTL to wrap state updates in
calls automatically. React 19 and Server Components require testing in server rendering environments for full coverage — Next.js and Remix both ship testing utilities that handle the RSC layer.
The React Roadmap: What Is Coming Next
As of 2025, the React team's publicly discussed priorities are:
The compiler that auto-memoises components is in opt-in production use at Meta. General availability is expected to remove the need for
React 19's preload APIs are the foundation; further work on coordinating CSS, font, and image loading with Suspense is ongoing.
The boundary between Server and Client Components is the biggest DX friction point in the RSC model. The React team is actively working on better error messages and tooling.

The React team has stated they are studying fine-grained reactivity (like Angular Signals and SolidJS) but have not announced a signals primitive for React. Any such addition would be additive and backwards-compatible.
The consistent theme across these items is the same as it has been since 2013: React's core model stays stable, and new capabilities are layered on top without breaking existing code.
Concurrent Rendering: What It Feels Like in Practice
The practical effect of concurrent rendering is subtle when things are working correctly — that is the point. Here is a concrete before-and-after scenario:
A user types in a search box. Each keystroke triggers

, which synchronously re-renders a list of 10,000 items. If each item takes 0.1ms to render, the total render blocks the browser for 1 second. The user's next keystroke is ignored until the render finishes. The input feels sluggish or unresponsive.

The same keystroke immediately updates the input value (urgent update), then schedules the list re-render as a non-urgent transition. React interleaves the list rendering in idle browser frames. If another keystroke arrives, React

the in-progress list render, processes the keystroke immediately, then resumes or replaces the list render. The input never feels sluggish, and the list eventually shows the latest query without intermediate flash.

) is a common UX pattern — show the user stale results while the fresh render is pending, rather than showing a spinner that interrupts flow. This pattern was impossible to implement cleanly before concurrent rendering.
: class components, Virtual DOM, lifecycle methods, Fiber rewrite in v16.
: Hooks (v16.8) replaced class components as the recommended pattern; custom hooks enable reusable stateful logic.
: Concurrent Mode (v18) and React Server Components (v19) extend React's model to server-driven rendering.
React maintains strong backwards compatibility — class components still work in v19.
For new projects in 2025, use React 19 with functional components and hooks.
